turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kagan has the opinion of the Court in case 16-1432, Sveen v. Melin.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: This case is about a Minnesota statute that addresses where a person's life insurance proceeds go when he dies. Imagine that a person buys a life insurance policy and names his spouse as beneficiary, then he gets divorced but doesn't change his beneficiary designation. Where should the insurance money go when he passes away? Should it go to his ex-spouse or should it go to someone he listed as a contingent beneficiary like his children or his parents. Minnesota made a judgment that most people who got divorced don't want their ex-spouses to receive their insurance money. So the Legislature enacted a law to prevent that from happening. The law provides that a divorce automatically revokes the designation of a spouse in a life insurance policy or similar asset when that divorce occurs, so the money instead would go to a contingent beneficiary. But if the insured person doesn't want that result, if for whatever reason, he wants to enrich his ex-spouse, he can restore her beneficiary status by sending a simple notice to his insurance company. Now, everyone agrees that this law is perfectly fine for all future life insurance policies. The question here is whether the law can be applied to a policy that was purchased before the law was enacted. If we want to use some legal lingo, the question is whether the law can be applied retroactively. So here are the facts, Mark Sveen purchased a life insurance policy, named his wife his beneficiary, and his children from a prior marriage as contingent beneficiaries. This is all before the law I told you about came on the books. Then, Sveen divorced his wife but without changing the beneficiary designation, and then he passed away. By the time he died, Minnesota had passed the law, does it apply to his insurance policy? If the law applies, his children get the money because the designation of his ex-wife is automatically revoked upon divorce. If the law doesn't apply, there is no automatic revocation and his ex-wife gets the money. Sveen's ex-wife, her name is Melin argued that the law couldn't constitutionally apply to a pre-existing insurance policy. Her argument was based on the Contracts Clause which prohibits states from impairing the obligation of contracts. The insurance policy is just a contract. So Melin argued the Contracts Clause prevents Minnesota from interfering with it. We hold today that the Contracts Clause does not prohibit the application of Minnesota's law to a contract made before it came on the books. The Contracts Clause puts limits on the power of states to disrupt pre-existing contractual arrangements but it doesn't prohibit every state law affecting those old contracts. Here in the application of the law is valid for three reasons. First, the law is designed to reflect a policyholder's intent and so to support rather than impair the insurance contract. The law is based on the reasonable assumption that most people who got divorced, don't want their former spouses to get their insurance money. So the law mostly honors rather than undermines the intent of the contracting party. Second, the law is unlikely to disrupt any settled expectations of that party. A person buying insurance can't reasonably rely on a beneficiary designation remaining in place after a divorce. That's because divorce courts have very substantial power to do whatever they want with beneficiary designations in the process of dividing assets. So no one can truly expect such a designation to survive a marital breakdown. And third, Minnesota's law supplies only what we would call a default rule; in other words, a rule that the policyholder can easily undo. The law puts in place a presumption about what the policyholder wants. But if that presumption is wrong in an individual case, the insured can reverse it simply by sending a change of beneficiary form to his insurer. That effectively cancels the law's operation. Today's holding is not novel. In cases going back to the 1800s, the court has upheld statutes requiring a contracting party to complete or record certain kinds of paperwork in order to retain contract benefits. Minnesota's law does essentially the same thing by requiring a person who wants to retain his ex-spouse as his beneficiary to send a form to his insurance company. The burden here is no greater than in those old cases. We accordingly reverse the Eighth Circuit which came out the other way. Justice Gorsuch has filed a dissenting opinion.
